content.thinking:
The openssl-toolbox pod doesn't exist yet. I need to create it. Let me plan the approach:

1. Create an openssl-toolbox pod for certificate generation
2. Generate CA certificate and key
3. Generate node certificates for each CockroachDB pod
4. Generate client certificate for the root user
5. Create Kubernetes secrets with the certificates
6. Update the StatefulSet to:
   - Mount the certificate secrets as volumes
   - Change `--insecure` to `--certs-dir=/cockroach/cockroach-certs`
   - Update health probes from HTTP to HTTPS
7. Restart the pods with a rolling update

For the certificate structure, I need a self-signed CA cert, node certificates signed by that CA with SANs for all the pod hostnames, and a client certificate for the root user. All these files go into `/cockroach/cockroach-certs/` with the CA cert, node cert, and node key in the expected locations.

The file permissions matter too—the CA and client certs need 644 permissions while the private keys need 600. I'll use the openssl-toolbox pod to generate all the certificates, then create Kubernetes secrets from them. The approach is to spin up a utility pod for certificate generation, create the CA, node, and client certificates with the proper SANs, then extract them into Kubernetes secrets. After that, I'll update the StatefulSet to mount those secrets and switch from insecure mode to using the certificates directory, plus convert the health checks to HTTPS.

input.command:
kubectl -n cockroachdb exec openssl-toolbox -- sh -c '
mkdir -p /certs && cd /certs

# 1. Generate CA key and self-signed cert
openssl genrsa -out ca.key 2048
openssl req -new -x509 -key ca.key -out ca.crt -days 3650 \
  -subj "/CN=CockroachDB CA/O=CockroachDB"

# 2. Generate node key and CSR
openssl genrsa -out node.key 2048
openssl req -new -key node.key -out node.csr \
  -subj "/CN=node/O=CockroachDB"

# 3. Create SAN extension file for node cert
cat > node_ext.cnf <<EXTEOF
[v3_req]
subjectAltName = @alt_names
[alt_names]
DNS.1 = localhost
DNS.2 = crdb-cluster-0.crdb-cluster.cockroachdb.svc.cluster.local
DNS.3 = crdb-cluster-1.crdb-cluster.cockroachdb.svc.cluster.local
DNS.4 = crdb-cluster-2.crdb-cluster.cockroachdb.svc.cluster.local
DNS.5 = crdb-cluster-0.crdb-cluster.cockroachdb
DNS.6 = crdb-cluster-1.crdb-cluster.cockroachdb
DNS.7 = crdb-cluster-2.crdb-cluster.cockroachdb
DNS.8 = crdb-cluster-0
DNS.9 = crdb-cluster-1
DNS.10 = crdb-cluster-2
DNS.11 = crdb-cluster.cockroachdb.svc.cluster.local
DNS.12 = crdb-cluster-public.cockroachdb.svc.cluster.local
IP.1 = 127.0.0.1
EXTEOF

# 4. Sign the node cert with CA
openssl x509 -req -in node.csr -CA ca.crt -CAkey ca.key -CAcreateserial \
  -out node.crt -days 3650 -extensions v3_req -extfile node_ext.cnf

# 5. Generate client (root) key and cert
openssl genrsa -out client.root.key 2048
openssl req -new -key client.root.key -out client.root.csr \
  -subj "/CN=root/O=CockroachDB"
openssl x509 -req -in client.root.csr -CA ca.crt -CAkey ca.key -CAcreateserial \
  -out client.root.crt -days 3650

echo "=== Certs generated ==="
ls -la /certs/
' 2>&1

# Extract cert contents from the pod
CA_CRT=$(kubectl -n cockroachdb exec openssl-toolbox -- cat /certs/ca.crt)
NODE_CRT=$(kubectl -n cockroachdb exec openssl-toolbox -- cat /certs/node.crt)
NODE_KEY=$(kubectl -n cockroachdb exec openssl-toolbox -- cat /certs/node.key)
CLIENT_CRT=$(kubectl -n cockroachdb exec openssl-toolbox -- cat /certs/client.root.crt)
CLIENT_KEY=$(kubectl -n cockroachdb exec openssl-toolbox -- cat /certs/client.root.key)

# Create node cert secret (same cert used by all 3 nodes)
kubectl -n cockroachdb create secret generic crdb-node-certs \
  --from-literal=ca.crt="$CA_CRT" \
  --from-literal=node.crt="$NODE_CRT" \
  --from-literal=node.key="$NODE_KEY"

# Create client root cert secret
kubectl -n cockroachdb create secret generic crdb-client-root \
  --from-literal=ca.crt="$CA_CRT" \
  --from-literal=client.root.crt="$CLIENT_CRT" \
  --from-literal=client.root.key="$CLIENT_KEY"

echo "Secrets created:"
kubectl -n cockroachdb get secrets

The pod is running but the readiness probe is failing with HTTP 503. This means the pod started but readiness isn't passing. 

The issue is that pods 0 and 1 haven't been restarted yet (they're still on the old insecure config), but pod 2 has been updated and is now running in secure mode. The cluster may not be able to form quorum because the nodes can't communicate with each other (0 and 1 are insecure, 2 is secure).

Wait, looking at the age - pods 0 and 1 still show their original age of 7m28s while pod 2 shows 114s. 

StatefulSets roll updates from highest to lowest ordinal, so pod 2 got updated first and switched to TLS mode, but pods 0 and 1 are still in insecure mode and can't talk to it. The update hasn't progressed to them yet.

The real blocker is that pod 2's readiness probe is failing because CockroachDB can't mix insecure and secure modes in the same cluster. Pod 2 tries to connect to the insecure pods 0 and 1, which fails. CockroachDB doesn't support live migration from insecure to secure—the entire cluster needs to be shut down and restarted in secure mode.

I need to scale down the StatefulSet completely to fix this. The issue is that pod 2 starts in TLS mode but can't join the cluster because pods 0 and 1 are still running insecure, creating a mode mismatch that leaves pod 2 stuck. I need to force a complete restart by scaling to zero then back up, but first let me check pod 2's logs to see if the 503 readiness failure is due to the cluster join issue or just the node still initializing.

# Poll until all 3 pods are Running (up to ~2 minutes)
for i in $(seq 1 24); do
  STATUS=$(kubectl -n cockroachdb get pods crdb-cluster-0 crdb-cluster-1 crdb-cluster-2 \
    --no-headers 2>/dev/null | awk '{print $3}' | sort -u)
  READY=$(kubectl -n cockroachdb get pods crdb-cluster-0 crdb-cluster-1 crdb-cluster-2 \
    --no-headers 2>/dev/null | awk '{print $2}' | sort -u)
  echo "[$i] Status: $STATUS  Ready: $READY"
  if kubectl -n cockroachdb get pods crdb-cluster-0 crdb-cluster-1 crdb-cluster-2 \
      --no-headers 2>/dev/null | awk '{print $2}' | grep -v "1/1" > /dev/null 2>&1; then
    [ $i -eq 24 ] && break
    sleep 5
  else
    echo "All pods 1/1 Ready!"
    break
  fi
done
